skill.name: scaffolding-fastapi-dapr
skill.description: Scaffold production-oriented FastAPI backends that use SQLModel, Dapr sidecars, JWT/JWKS authentication, async database access, and testable service patterns.
skill.summary: Create FastAPI service scaffolds with SQLModel, Dapr integration, authentication, testing, and production patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and script patterns, but most are Markdown examples or SQLModel method calls rather than hidden execution. One confirmed concern remains: a Dapr setup command pipes a remote GitHub install script into bash, which creates supply-chain risk if run automatically. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent was found in the reviewed context.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The Dapr quick start recommends downloading an install script from GitHub and piping it directly to bash. This is a real supply-chain execution risk if an agent or user runs it without inspecting the script, although it appears to be a standard Dapr setup example rather than malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and executable: curl retrieves a remote script and pipes it to bash. Context shows legitimate installation guidance, so the risk is confirmed but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Documented Local Commands May Be Run By Agents
medium_findings.description: The skill includes setup and runtime commands for uv, uvicorn, Dapr, pytest, and similar tools. These commands are presented as documentation examples, not hidden execution, but automated use should ask before installing dependencies or starting services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown code blocks and align with the skill purpose. The risk is operational rather than covert because no file automatically executes them.

medium_findings.title: Database And Auth Environment Variables In Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples read DATABASE_URL and authentication URLs from environment variables and show placeholder database connection strings. This is normal backend configuration, but generated projects should avoid committing real secrets and should avoid logging sensitive connection details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly access database and auth configuration. The values are placeholders or environment reads, so this is a configuration hygiene concern rather than exfiltration.

low_findings.title: SQLModel session.exec Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Several high-severity script and process-exec detections refer to SQLModel session.exec calls. In this context, exec is a database query method, not Python dynamic code execution or process execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context uses SQLModel sessions and select statements. No evidence shows Python exec(), shell execution, or user-controlled code evaluation at these locations.

low_findings.title: Localhost And JWKS URLs Are Expected Service Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are used for Dapr sidecar calls, JWKS discovery, localhost defaults, and documentation examples. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates data to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs support expected backend authentication and local development behavior. No suspicious external collection endpoint or credential transfer pattern was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The reviewed authentication examples use JWT verification with RS256 and JWKS. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, or intentionally weak cryptography in the cited authentication snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected snippets show RS256 JWT verification and key retrieval. Static weak-crypto hits appear to be keyword-based matches rather than confirmed weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Well Known JWKS Path Misclassified As Hidden File Access
low_findings.description: The hidden-file finding points to a .well-known JWKS URL path. This is a standard HTTP discovery path for public keys, not local hidden file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is an HTTP JWKS discovery URL string. It does not read a dotfile from disk or access hidden filesystem paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The Dapr quick start recommends downloading an install script from GitHub and piping it directly to bash. This is a real supply-chain execution risk if an agent or user runs it without inspecting the script, although it appears to be a standard Dapr setup example rather than malicious behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and executable: curl retrieves a remote script and pipes it to bash. Context shows legitimate installation guidance, so the risk is confirmed but not evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Building a production FastAPI service requires database, authentication, Dapr, and testing patterns to work together. This skill gives structured backend scaffolding guidance for SQLModel, JWT/JWKS, Dapr pub/sub, jobs, and async tests.
actual_capabilities: Creates FastAPI project patterns with SQLModel models, CRUD routes, and dependency injection.
Guides async database setup for PostgreSQL and Neon-style connection strings.
Adds JWT and JWKS authentication examples for protected API endpoints.
Shows Dapr pub/sub subscriptions, event publishing, state access, and job callback patterns.
Provides repository, service, structured logging, health check, and pytest testing patterns.
Includes a verification script that checks required reference files are present.
limitations: It provides scaffolding guidance and examples, not a complete generated application by itself.
Some commands install dependencies or start local services and should be reviewed before execution.
Dapr Jobs API examples may need version checks because the API is marked alpha in the reference text.
Authentication examples require a real identity provider and JWKS endpoint supplied by the project.

use_cases.title: New Backend Service
use_cases.description: Scaffold a FastAPI service with SQLModel models, CRUD endpoints, async sessions, and health checks.

use_cases.title: Event Driven Microservice
use_cases.description: Add Dapr pub/sub handlers, CloudEvent unwrapping, event publishing, and sidecar runtime patterns.

use_cases.title: Testable API Foundation
use_cases.description: Create pytest fixtures, dependency overrides, repository layers, and async client tests for FastAPI routes.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic API Scaffold
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to scaffold a FastAPI task API with SQLModel models, create and list endpoints, and a simple database session dependency.

prompt_templates.title: Add Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add JWT and JWKS authentication to my FastAPI API, including a current-user dependency and protected route examples.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Dapr Pub/Sub
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Dapr pub/sub support for task-created events, including subscription discovery, CloudEvent handling, and event publishing.

prompt_templates.title: Build Production Test Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to organize my FastAPI backend with repositories, services, structured logging, health checks, and async pytest fixtures.

output_examples.input: Scaffold a task API with SQLModel and FastAPI.
output_examples.output: A backend outline with models, route handlers, session dependencies, create and list workflows, and validation boundaries.

output_examples.input: Add Dapr messaging to an existing FastAPI service.
output_examples.output: A pub/sub plan with subscription discovery, CloudEvent unwrapping, handler responsibilities, publishing flow, and sidecar run notes.

output_examples.input: Prepare the service for production testing.

best_practices: Review installation commands before running them, especially commands that download and execute remote scripts.
Keep real database URLs, tokens, and JWKS endpoints in environment-specific secret stores.
Start with a small CRUD route and test fixture before adding Dapr messaging or scheduled jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not commit real connection strings or JWT tokens into generated examples or test files.
Do not treat SQLModel session.exec calls as dynamic Python execution.
Do not run Dapr sidecars or dependency installation commands without checking local environment impact.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps scaffold FastAPI backends with SQLModel, Dapr messaging, JWT authentication, testing, and production structure.

faq.question: Does it create a complete application automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and scaffolding guidance that an assistant can apply to a project.

faq.question: Can it work without Dapr?
faq.answer: Yes. The FastAPI, SQLModel, authentication, and testing patterns can be used without Dapr-specific features.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: Most detections are documentation examples, but one setup command pipes a remote install script into bash.

faq.question: Does it include authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes JWT and JWKS verification patterns for protected FastAPI endpoints.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
